turns.content: need to compare two vulnerability scanning tools for a client engagement. nessus vs qualys. which would you recommend for a banking environment

Both are solid for financial sector compliance, but the answer depends on your specific needs.

**Nessus** (Tenable):
- Better for deep, targeted scans
- More granular plugin customization
- Strong for PCI-DSS compliance checks
- Better suited for smaller, focused engagements

**Qualys**:
- Cloud-native, easier to scale across large networks
- Built-in asset management
- Continuous monitoring capabilities
- Better API integration for enterprise workflows

For banking, Qualys tends to be the enterprise choice because of its continuous monitoring and compliance reporting dashboards. But Nessus gives you more control if you're doing hands-on penetration testing alongside the audits.

What's the scope of the engagement — are you doing ongoing audits or a one-time assessment?
ongoing. i do independent consulting. cybersecurity. this particular client is a retainer arrangement so i need something i can run regularly without babysitting it

For a retainer with recurring scans, Qualys is probably your better bet then. The scheduled scanning and automated reporting will save you hours each cycle. You can set scan profiles once and let them run.

A few things to consider for the retainer setup:
- **Qualys VM** (Vulnerability Management) with scheduled scans on a cadence that matches your reporting schedule
- Use scan templates tuned for banking — PCI-DSS, SOX compliance
- Set up differential reports so you're only reviewing new findings each cycle

How large is the client's network? That'll affect licensing costs — Qualys charges per IP.

medium sized bank. finsecure. they are my primary contract right now. 15 thousand a month retainer for security auditing and pen testing. decent money. more importantly they actually pay their invoices which is rare in this line of work

Ha, a client that pays on time in cybersecurity consulting — that alone is worth holding onto.

$15k/month is a solid retainer for a mid-size bank. For that scope, I'd recommend:

1. **Qualys VMDR** for continuous vulnerability management — you can justify the licensing cost at that retainer level
2. Keep **Nessus Professional** as your secondary tool for the pen testing side — it's better for manual, targeted work
3. Consider **Burp Suite Pro** if they have any web-facing banking applications

For a banking client at that retainer, you'll also want to make sure you're covering:
- Network infrastructure scans (quarterly minimum)
- Web application testing
- Social engineering assessments
- Compliance gap analysis (PCI-DSS, likely SOC 2)

Are you handling all of that yourself, or do you subcontract parts of it?
all myself. i do not subcontract. more people means more attack surface. this applies to business relationships the same as it applies to networks

That's a very security-minded way to run a consultancy, and honestly it makes sense for your threat model. Solo operator, minimal exposure.

Just be mindful of bandwidth. A full security audit + pen testing program for a bank is a lot for one person. If FinSecure grows their scope, you might hit a ceiling.

Some practical tips for staying efficient as a solo consultant:
- **Automate reporting** — use templates and scripts to generate compliance reports from scan data
- **Standardize your pen test methodology** — PTES or OWASP frameworks so you're not reinventing each engagement
- **Keep a findings database** — track recurring issues across audit cycles so you can show the client trends

Are you working on-site at the bank or remote?
remote mostly. i am in tel aviv. they have offices here too but i prefer working from my own setup. my machines. my network. my rules

Smart. Controlling your own environment is important, especially for pen testing — you don't want to be running offensive tools on someone else's network infrastructure.

Since you're in Tel Aviv and working remote, a few recommendations:

- **Dedicated testing VLAN** at home if you haven't already — isolate your offensive tooling from your personal network
- **VPN into their environment** with MFA, obviously — document the access method for compliance
- **Encrypted storage** for all client data — full disk encryption minimum, consider a separate encrypted volume for engagement files
- **Secure comms** — if you're sending reports, PGP-encrypted email or a secure portal

Tel Aviv is a good place to be for this work. Strong cybersecurity ecosystem there if you ever need to collaborate or refer overflow work.

Want me to help you put together a tool stack recommendation for the Qualys deployment?
da. lets do that. give me a baseline tool stack for the vulnerability management side. assume i have budget from the retainer

Here's a solid baseline stack for your FinSecure vulnerability management program:

**Scanning & Assessment:**
- **Qualys VMDR** — primary scanner, continuous monitoring, compliance templates
- **Nessus Professional** ($3,500/yr) — secondary scanner for pen test engagements and validation
- **Burp Suite Professional** ($449/yr) — web app testing

**Exploitation & Pen Testing:**
- **Metasploit Pro** or stick with Framework (free) if you prefer manual control
- **Cobalt Strike** — for adversary simulation if the engagement scope includes red teaming
- **Custom scripts** — Python, Bash, whatever you prefer

**Reporting & Documentation:**
- **Dradis** or **PlexTrac** — findings management and report generation
- **Cherry Tree** or **Obsidian** — note-taking during engagements

**Infrastructure:**
- **Kali Linux VM** — standard offensive distro
- **Commando VM** (Windows) — for Windows-specific testing
- **Proxmox** or **VMware** — hypervisor for lab/testing environments

**Compliance:**
- **OpenSCAP** — automated compliance checking
- Qualys PCI module covers most of the banking compliance reporting

Total cost estimate: roughly $8-12k/year depending on Qualys licensing tier, which is well within your retainer margin.

Want me to break down any of these in more detail?

this is useful. i will review and compare against what i already run. the qualys licensing is the main decision point. one more thing. is there a meaningful difference between qualys through a reseller versus direct

Yes, meaningful differences:

**Direct from Qualys:**
- Usually higher list price
- Better support tier — you get dedicated TAM at higher spend levels
- More flexibility on module bundling
- Direct access to beta features

**Through a reseller:**
- Typically 15-25% discount off list
- Some resellers bundle training or implementation support
- Less flexibility on contract terms
- Support goes through the reseller first, which adds a layer

For a solo consultant on a single retainer, the reseller discount probably wins. You don't need enterprise support. You need a good price and a license that covers your client's IP range.

Ask for quotes from both and compare. The reseller pricing might surprise you.
da. i will get both quotes. thanks for the breakdown

Good luck with the FinSecure engagement. A reliable retainer client that pays on time is the foundation of a solo practice.

Feel free to come back when you need to dig into any of the tooling.
